text: The surgeon uses a PK device and unipolar scissors to safely cut through filmy adhesions in the abdominal cavity, despite the device making an error sound due to insufficient tissue for a secure seal. The adhesion is completed with minimal bleeding. The surgeon then explores the omentum, moving it left to right to ensure no underlying bowel is present before sealing the area. The omentum, composed mostly of fatty tissue, may also trigger an error sound with the PK device, but the surgeon proceeds with cutting using unipolar scissors, avoiding significant bleeding as no major blood vessels are visible.

The surgeon approaches the last few adhesions near the anterior abdominal wall, using blunt exploration to ensure no significant structures like bowel are behind the dissection area. A thicker adhesion between the fascia and omentum is encountered, sealed with the PK device, and dissected with unipolar scissors without bleeding.

The surgeon completes the takedown of the final adhesion and proceeds to the hysterectomy. The right side of the periuterine tissue is skeletonized, and the creation of the right bladder flap begins. Vessels on the right periuterine side are grasped, sealed, and doubly sealed above and below before transection to ensure hemostasis.

The surgeon uses open unipolar scissors to carefully dissect the superficial perineum, gradually deepening the incision to open the bladder flap on the right side. This technique exposes the blood vessels at the edge, which are then secured with the PK device before transection. The video shows dense adhesions on the vesicle uterine peritoneum, requiring meticulous dissection to manage the vasculature while progressing with the bladder flap takedown.

The surgeon moves to the left-hand side, dissecting the periuterine tissues down to the cervical isthmus. Using a left fenestrated bipolar instrument, the surgeon pushes under the colpotomy cup, outlining its edge and painting the serosal tissue to detach the adhesed bladder from the uterus. Whitish tissue is exposed with controlled bleeding, preparing for the colpotomy incision.

The surgeon uses unipolar scissors to paint the vesicle uterine perineum on the right side, pushing it over and under the colpotomy cup. The outline of the cup becomes visible as superficial tissue is removed, facilitating control of minor bleeding and preparing for the colpotomy incision.

The surgeon prepares to make the colpotomy incision, with the entire edge of the colpotomy cup visible under a white sheen. The green edge of the cup becomes visible as the colpotomy begins, indicating the start of the incision.
